title: Conclusions in observational studies versus experiments
description: Practice determining what conclusions we can draw from a study based on it being an observational study or an experiment.
topic_path: Conclusions in observational studies versus experiments

assessment_items.question_content:
A recent study showed that players of a certain video game tend to perform better if they take breaks. The study involved analyzing data from $242{,}000$ matches played by $16{,}665$ players of League of Legends, a popular online game. The data showed that players who took regular breaks, on average, performed better than those who did not.

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign players to take breaks—each player decided how often they would take a break. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say something like "taking breaks caused the players to perform better."

We can say that there is an *association* between between taking breaks and better performance.

###Answer

We can conclude that there was an association between taking breaks and better performance, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

choices.content: Taking regular breaks caused the players to perform better.
Performing better wore out the players and caused them to take more breaks.
Any player in this study can perform better by taking more breaks.
For any two players in this study, the one who took more breaks also had better performance.
There was an association between taking breaks and better performance, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

assessment_items.question_content:
A recent study examined $323$ healthy women who wore watch-like devices that tracked their sleep each night. The women with sleep problems (difficultly falling asleep and waking up throughout the night) were significantly more likely to have high blood pressure than women without those sleep problems.

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign women to certain sleep patterns—their sleep was simply monitored. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say something like "sleep problems caused higher blood pressure."

We can say that there is an *association* between between sleep problems and high blood pressure.

###Answer

We can conclude that there was an association between sleep problems and high blood pressure, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

choices.content: Having sleep problems caused the women to have higher blood pressure.
Higher blood pressure caused the women to have sleep problems.
Any woman in this study with sleep problems also had high blood pressure.
For any two women in this study, the one who has more sleep problems has higher blood pressure.
There was an association between sleep problems and high blood pressure, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

assessment_items.question_content:
The superintendent of a large school district is curious about the relationship between students' grades and their participation in extracurricular activities. They take a random sample of $500$ students and find that students with more participation in extracurricular activities are far more likely to have high grades than students with little or no participation.

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign students to extracurricular activities—their participation was simply monitored. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say something like "extracurricular participation caused higher grades."

We can say that there is an *association* between between extracurricular participation and grades.

###Answer

We can conclude that there was an association between extracurricular participation and grades, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

choices.content: For any two students in this study, the one who participated more in extracurriculars had higher grades.
Any student in this study who participated in extracurriculars had high grades.
There was an association between extracurricular participation and grades, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.
Extracurricular participation caused the students to have higher grades.
Higher grades caused the students to have higher extracurricular participation.

assessment_items.question_content:
A survey of over $7{,}000$ randomly selected employees in $15$ nations recently showed that employees who work in a digital workplace tended to be happier than those who don't work in a digital workplace.

**Can we conclude that working in a digital workplace *caused* employees to be happier? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign employees to a digital or non-digital workplace. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say that the digital workplace is the cause of their happiness.

We can say that there is an *association* between digital workplaces and happiness.

###Answer

No, we cannot conclude that working in a digital workplace *caused* employees to be happier, because this wasn't an experiment.

choices.content: Yes, because the sample size was large
Yes, because the employees were randomly selected
Yes, because the employees were randomly assigned
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers were interested in the relationship between farmers’ approach to their cows and the cows’ milk yield. After surveying a random sample of $120$ farmers, the researchers found that on farms where cows were called by name, milk yield was $258$ liters higher on average than on farms where this was not the case.

**Can we conclude that calling the cows by name *caused* the cows to produce more milk? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign cows to be called by their name or not. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say that the calling cows by name causes them to produce more milk.

We can say that there is an *association* between calling the cows by name and their milk production.

###Answer

No, we cannot conclude that calling the cows by name *caused* the cows to produce more milk, because this wasn't an experiment.

choices.content: Yes, because the sample size was large
Yes, because the farmers were randomly selected
Yes, because the cows were randomly assigned
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
Researchers studying meditation contacted over $1{,}000$ randomly selected adults in $10$ nations. The results showed that those who meditated on a regular basis were less likely to suffer from stress than adults who meditate less regularly or not at all.

**Can we conclude that meditating *caused* the lower stress? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question didn't randomly assign adults to meditate or not. That makes this an **observational study**, so we cannot make a causal conclusion and say that the meditation is the cause of their lower stress.

We can say that there is an *association* between meditation and stress.

###Answer

No, we cannot conclude that meditating *caused* lower stress, because this wasn't an experiment.

choices.content: Yes, because the sample size was large
Yes, because the adults were randomly selected
Yes, because the adults were randomly assigned
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
Roshanda was curious if students in her class would complete a maze faster if fast-paced music was playing in the background. She randomly assigned each student in her class to one of three groups: fast-paced music, slow-paced music, or no music. She timed each student as they individually completed a maze with their corresponding music (or no music) in the background. Students who worked with the fast-paced music, on average, completed the maze significantly faster than students in the other groups.

**Can we conclude that the fast-paced music *caused* her classmates to finish the maze faster? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned students to one of three treatment groups: fast-paced music, slow-paced music, or no music. That makes this an **experiment**, so we can make a causal conclusion and say that the fast-paced music *caused* faster finishing times.

###Answer

Yes, we can conclude that the fast-paced music *caused* her classmates to finish the maze faster, because the students were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups.

choices.content: Yes, because the study included each student in her class
Yes, because the students were randomly selected
Yes, because the students were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
Jillian has been updating the design of a media streaming website. She wonders if users will spend more time on the site with the new design, so she designs a study. She starts with a random sample of $1{,}000$ users and randomly assigns half of them to the old design and half of them to the new design. After a week, she notices that users with the new design spend significantly longer on the site than the users with the old design.

**Can we conclude that the new design *caused* users to spend more time on the site? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned some users to the old design and some to the new design. That makes this an **experiment**, so we can make a causal conclusion and say that the new design *caused* users to spend more time on the site.

###Answer

Yes, we can conclude that the new design *caused* users to spend more time on the site, because the users were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups.

choices.content: Yes, because the sample size was large
Yes, because the users were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups
Yes, because the users were randomly selected
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
Andrey works at a call center, selling insurance over the phone. While debating which greeting he should use when calling potential customers — “Howdy!” or “Hiya!” — he decided to conduct a small study.

For his subsequent $500$ calls, he chose one of the greetings randomly by flipping a coin, and used the same script for his sale otherwise. Then, he compared the percentage of calls he succeeded in selling insurance using each greeting. He noticed that he was significantly more likely to make a sale in calls that started with "Hiya!"

**Can we conclude that the greeting *caused* the difference in sales? Why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned customers to one of the two greetings. That makes this an **experiment**, and since the sales script was the same otherwise, we can make a causal conclusion and say that the "Hiya!" greeting *caused* the difference in sales.

###Answer

Yes, we can conclude that the greeting *caused* the difference in sales, because the calls were each randomly assigned a greeting.

choices.content: Yes, because the sample size was large
Yes, because the calls were each randomly assigned a greeting
Yes, because the customers were randomly selected
Not necessarily, because the sample size was too small
Not necessarily, because this wasn't an experiment

assessment_items.question_content:
A grocery store wonders if their sales will be better if they play ambient music or no music at all. Each day for $2$ months, the manager flips a coin to determine whether or not the store will play music that day. At the end of the $2$ months, the manager finds that, on average, sales were significantly better on days where music was playing.

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned some days to have music and some days to have no music. That makes this an **experiment**, so we can make a causal conclusion and say that playing music *caused* better sales.

###Answer

We can conclude that playing music caused better sales.

choices.content: Playing music caused better sales.
Better sales caused the manager to play music.
Any day where music was played had higher sales than a day where no music was played.
Any store could increase sales by playing the same music.
There was an association between music and sales, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

assessment_items.question_content:
A majority of people who receive surveys in the mail don't respond. Researchers were curious which type of reward would be most effective: send someone $\$10$ after they mail a completed survey back, or send someone $\$5$ with a survey as an advanced "thank you" that they can keep whether or not they participate.

The researchers chose a random sample of $1200$ adults and randomly assigned each of them to one of three groups: $\$10$ after, $\$5$ in advance, or no reward at all. They found that people in the $\$5$ group were significantly more likely to send the survey back than people in either of the other groups.

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 3]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 4]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned some people to each reward type. That makes this an **experiment**, so we can make a causal conclusion and say that the $\$5$ reward *caused* better rates of survey completion.

###Answer

We can conclude that the $\$5$ in advance caused the difference in survey completion.

choices.content: Survey completion caused the difference in reward type.
The $\$5$ in advance caused the difference in survey completion.
Any person given $\$5$ in advance will complete this survey.
Any amount of money given in advance would lead to a higher response rate than any amount of money given after.
There was an association between reward type and survey completion, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alejandro owns a restaurant where he sells pizza by the slice. He wonders if customers prefer their pepperoni on top of the cheese or under the cheese. Every day for a week, he prepares both types of pizza. Every time a customer orders a slice of pepperoni pizza, he flips a coin to determine whether he'll give the customer a slice with pepperoni on top or under the cheese.

He then asked each customer to rate their pizza. At the end of the week, he found that customers who received pepperoni on top rated their pizza significantly better on average than customers who received pepperoni under the cheese. 

**What conclusion can they draw from this study?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers *do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment*. If there is a relationship between variables in this sort of study, we can say there is an *association* between those variables, but we *cannot* make causal conclusions. Other variables may be causing the relationship we see, or the causation could be reversed. [[☃ explanation 1]]

In an **experiment**, researchers *assign participants to groups* and impose a treatment on at least one of those groups. We can make causal conclusions from a well-designed experiment, because random assignment reasonably balances other variables between the treatment and control groups. [[☃ explanation 2]]

In this question, were the individuals involved randomly assigned to treatment groups?

The study described in this question randomly assigned some customers to have pepperoni on top, and some to have pepperoni under the cheese. That makes this an **experiment**, so we can make a causal conclusion and say that pepperoni placement *caused* the difference in customer ratings.

###Answer

We can conclude that pepperoni placement caused the difference in customer ratings.

choices.content: Customer ratings caused the difference in pepperoni placement.
Pepperoni placement caused the difference in customer ratings
Any customer would prefer pepperoni on top of the cheese.
Any other pizza restaurant should put their pepperoni on top of the cheese.
There was an association between pepperoni placement and customer rating, but it's not necessarily a causal relationship.
